does: This paper introduces VSRAQ, a post-training quantization method designed specifically for Mixture-of-Experts models. The core innovation addresses routing instability—the problem that small quantization errors can shift which experts are selected for each token, fundamentally altering computation. VSRAQ combines two complementary objectives: value alignment, which preserves the logit scores that determine expert selection, and structure alignment, which maintains the ordering and decision boundaries for top-k expert selection. By jointly optimizing these objectives during quantization, the method ensures that expert routing patterns remain consistent before and after quantization, preventing the quality degradation typical in quantized MoE models. The approach integrates cleanly into existing quantization frameworks and adds no inference-time overhead.
connects: This work builds on the rich literature of neural network quantization, extending post-training quantization techniques developed for dense models to the specialized case of MoE architectures. It responds to the recent emergence of MoE-based foundation models like Switch Transformers and GShard, which scale efficiently through sparse expert activation but introduce new quantization challenges absent in dense networks. The paper connects to prior work on preserving model behavior under quantization and leverages insights about how decision boundaries and logit magnitudes influence model outputs. It also engages with research on routing mechanisms in MoE models and the instability properties unique to sparse conditional computation. The value and structure alignment objectives draw conceptually from multi-objective optimization in quantization and from understanding how quantization noise propagates through routing decisions.
enables: This work enables practical deployment of large-scale MoE models on resource-constrained hardware by making quantization robust and reliable for these architectures. It opens directions for deploying sparse models efficiently across edge devices and data centers without the severe accuracy losses that naive quantization causes. The routing-consistency perspective may inspire similar structure-aware quantization methods for other conditional computation architectures or attention mechanisms where small perturbations significantly alter computation paths. Future work could extend VSRAQ to joint quantization of both weights and activations, explore dynamic quantization strategies that adapt per-token routing patterns, or investigate how routing-consistent quantization interacts with other MoE training techniques like expert load balancing and auxiliary losses.
